summary: My name is Jiro Oyama, born in 1925 in Boyle Heights, Los Angeles. I was the youngest child in my family, and I lived through the hardships of the Depression and World War II. I attended First Street Elementary, Hollenbeck Junior High, and Theodore Roosevelt High School before the war changed everything. My family owned a grocery store, and I witnessed firsthand the challenges we faced as Japanese Americans during that era.

I was born and raised in Boyle Heights, East Los Angeles, where my family moved several times but stayed within the neighborhood. Before the war, Boyle Heights was a diverse community with many Mexican American, Caucasian, and Asian families, including mine. We lived there through the 1930s and early 1940s, and it was the backdrop of my childhood and early schooling.

I graduated from First Street Elementary School when I was about 12 years old. I still have a picture of my graduation from there. The school was in Boyle Heights, and I remember that Asians made up a significant part of the student body, which surprised me at the time because I thought the area was mostly Mexican American and Caucasian.

After finishing elementary school, I went on to Hollenbeck Junior High School and then attended Theodore Roosevelt High School for ninth and tenth grades. Hollenbeck was part of my educational journey in Los Angeles before the war interrupted everything. It was during this time that my family owned a grocery store near the high school football field.

I attended Theodore Roosevelt High School for my ninth and tenth grades, right before the war started when I was 16. Our family grocery store was located right next to the high school football field, which was quite convenient. The war and the events following Pearl Harbor changed my high school experience drastically.

Both my parents were born and raised in Fukushima Prefecture, in the northern part of Japan. My father was born in 1888 and my mother in 1884. They left Fukushima and eventually met in Hawaii before moving to California, where they raised our family.

My parents met and married in Hawaii, where my father had moved in 1907 and my mother came in 1911 to teach Japanese at a Buddhist temple. They married in 1916 on Oahu, and it was there that the family started before moving to California. Hawaii was an important place in my family's early story.

My mother was a Japanese language teacher at a Buddhist temple in Hawaii, which was founded in 1908. It was there she met my father, and they married in 1916. This temple played a key role in their meeting and early life together before moving to the mainland.

After my parents married, the family first lived briefly in San Francisco before moving to Southern California due to the weather. San Francisco was a short stop in our migration story, but it was part of the transition from Hawaii to Los Angeles where we eventually settled.

Los Angeles was where my family spent most of their lives after moving from San Francisco. We lived in Boyle Heights, and my father worked various jobs including running a restaurant in the 7th Street Market area. Los Angeles was home for us through the Depression and up to the war years.

My father ran a restaurant located in the 7th Street Market in central Los Angeles for several years. This was a food distribution center where he worked until later taking a job as a cook on a fishing boat. The 7th Street Market was an important part of his working life before his passing in 1934.

My older brother started attending UCLA as a freshman just before the Pearl Harbor attack. Before classes, he helped out at our family grocery store by cutting meat. UCLA was a part of our lives until the war disrupted everything.

I was 16 years old when the Pearl Harbor attack happened on December 7, 1941. I remember hearing the news on the radio and feeling the weight of being Japanese American during that time. The next day at school, we stood on the football field listening to the President call it a 'day of infamy,' and I felt the burden of blame placed on me because of my heritage.

After Pearl Harbor, Executive Order 9066 was issued, and I remember reading the instructions about where we had to report and what we could bring. It was shocking to be told that all Japanese ancestry people, regardless of citizenship, had to comply with these orders. Our family began preparing to leave, trying to sell or get rid of belongings, not knowing what the future held.

After the Pearl Harbor attack, some customers stopped coming to our family grocery store, but a few new customers came, possibly to show sympathy. Some of these people would go out of their way to shop at our store instead of nearby Safeway stores, as a way to express empathy during those difficult times.

I was born and raised in the United States, in Los Angeles, and always considered myself American. Yet during World War II, despite my innocence, I was treated as an enemy because of my Japanese ancestry. The government's actions, including Executive Order 9066, stripped away our rights and forced us into a difficult and unjust situation.
